title: Visible expressions of Islam in public space affect Muslim immigrants' place integration

data_scope.study_type: two field experiments + two original surveys (primary data)
data_scope.behavioral_signal: GPS walking pace, acceleration, dwell time

core_conclusion: Contrary to the assumption that visible Islam drives Muslim immigrants to withdraw, in-situ exposure to embedded Islamic symbols increases approach-oriented behaviour (slower pace, lower acceleration, lingering, measured from GPS) and raises place attachment. Twin AR field experiments in Paris (N=151) and London (N=147) show medium effects (d=0.51-0.63, P<0.05), corroborated by surveys (N=403+400). The pattern holds across two opposite religious-visibility policy regimes.
why_it_matters: Provides randomized in-situ causal evidence on a charged policy debate (bans on visible religious display); measures behaviour via GPS rather than relying only on self-report; replicates across two contrasting policy regimes; introduces a reusable AR cue-injection field-experiment instrument; directly informs governance of religious diversity in public space.
why_nature: Couples the visible material city to a measured human outcome (movement + belonging); delivers causal identification in a real urban setting; generalizes across two policy contexts; is policy-translatable for immigrant integration; and contributes a novel AR+GPS measurement apparatus for urban social science.
added_value_to_my_research: Scale AR cue-injection to a city-wide passive-GPS panel -> micro-accessibility of belonging measure
Movement-based comfort index as a clean dependent variable for explainable spatial ML
Couple symbol exposure with measured segregation via network detection and spatial entropy
Fuse street-view visible features with GPS outcomes for foundation-model fine-tuning
Treat belonging as a behavioral impedance term in 2SFCA/R2SFCA health-access models
